hook: 83%. That's GPT-5.4's score beating human experts.
headline: GPT-5.4 Outscores Human Analysts at 83% on GDPVal
body: OpenAI dropped GPT-5.4 on March 5th, and the benchmark results tell a story the industry wasn't ready for. The model scored 83% on the GDPVal test for knowledge work -- the kind of spreadsheet analysis, data interpretation, and strategic reasoning that junior investment banking analysts do every day. On those specific tasks, it hit 87.3%.

But the headline number undersells the shift happening underneath. GPT-5.4 is 33% less likely to make factual errors compared to GPT-5.2, and overall responses contain 18% fewer mistakes. The Pro variant pushes even further: 89.3% on BrowseComp, 83.3% on ARC-AGI-2, and 38% on FrontierMath Tier 4 problems that stumped every previous model.

The coding story is equally aggressive. A 57.7% score on SWE-Bench Pro with lower latency means this isn't just smarter -- it's faster. OpenAI simultaneously shipped it to ChatGPT, the API, and Codex, signaling they view this as production-ready, not a research preview.

Here's what the benchmarks don't capture: the compounding effect. Each percentage point of reliability improvement doesn't just make the model better -- it unlocks entirely new categories of autonomous work that were previously too risky to delegate.
insight: The real disruption isn't the 83% score -- it's that GPT-5.4's 33% error reduction crosses the reliability threshold where enterprises can actually deploy AI for unsupervised decision-making. We just entered the 'good enough to trust' era.
caption:
The benchmark wars are over. The reliability war just started.

GPT-5.4 didn't just beat human experts on knowledge work tasks -- it did it while making 33% fewer errors than its predecessor. That error reduction matters more than any leaderboard score.

Why? Because enterprises don't deploy AI based on peak performance. They deploy based on worst-case reliability. And GPT-5.4 just moved the floor up significantly.

The question isn't whether AI can do your job. It's whether your job description just quietly changed.

What's the first knowledge work task you'd trust to an 83%-accurate AI? Drop it below.
